title: Milk
content: Milk is a nutrient-rich dairy drink from cows, buffalo, goats, or plant sources. Key nutrients: calcium, vitamin D (in fortified milk), high-quality protein, vitamin B12, riboflavin, and potassium. Health benefits: supports bone and teeth strength, muscle repair, and nerve function. Whole milk is higher in fat and calories than skim or low-fat milk, which offer the same protein and calcium with less saturated fat. People with lactose intolerance may experience bloating, gas, or diarrhea and can consider lactose-free milk or plant-based alternatives (soy, almond, oat) instead.
keywords: milk, dairy, calcium, vitamin D, lactose intolerance, bone health

title: Chicken
content: Chicken is a widely eaten lean poultry meat and a core protein source in non-vegetarian diets. Key nutrients: high-quality complete protein, vitamin B6, vitamin B12, niacin, selenium, and phosphorus, with relatively low fat when skinless (especially chicken breast). Health benefits: supports muscle growth and repair, is lower in saturated fat than most red meat, and is versatile across cuisines. Cooking method matters for healthiness — grilled, baked, or boiled chicken is far lower in added fat and calories than fried or heavily processed chicken.
keywords: chicken, poultry, protein, lean meat, non-vegetarian, vitamin B6, B12

title: Pizza
content: Pizza is a baked dish of a bread-like crust topped with tomato sauce, cheese, and various toppings (vegetables, meats, or both). Nutritionally it combines refined carbohydrates (crust), calcium and saturated fat (cheese), and whatever protein/fiber the toppings provide, along with typically high sodium content. It's best treated as a moderation food rather than a daily staple due to its calorie, sodium, and saturated fat load. Healthier versions use a whole-wheat or thin crust, part-skim cheese, and vegetable-heavy toppings, which increase fiber and micronutrients while lowering overall calories.
keywords: pizza, fast food, cheese, crust, sodium, moderation food

title: ADHD (Attention-Deficit/Hyperactivity Disorder)
content: ADHD (Attention-Deficit/Hyperactivity Disorder) is a neurodevelopmental condition affecting attention, impulse control, and activity regulation in both children and adults. Source: a combination of genetic and neurological factors affecting brain dopamine and norepinephrine regulation; not caused by diet, sugar, or parenting alone. Symptoms: difficulty sustaining focus, forgetfulness, impulsivity, restlessness, and hyperactivity. Prevention: there is no guaranteed way to prevent ADHD since genetics play a major role, but early diagnosis and intervention improve outcomes. Nutrition support: a balanced diet with adequate protein, iron, zinc, and omega-3 fatty acids (fish, flaxseed, walnuts) may support attention and mood; regular meal timing helps stabilize blood sugar and focus, while minimizing highly processed, high-sugar foods can reduce energy crashes; nutrition works alongside, not in place of, behavioral therapy and medical treatment.
keywords: ADHD, attention deficit, hyperactivity, focus, dopamine, omega-3
